items.title: Build the CodeGraph from a syntax-only Roslyn extractor
items.description: Construct the in-memory CodeGraph from a deterministic, syntax-only Roslyn pass that parses each file into a syntax tree and walks declarations. Do not build a Compilation, do not bind a semantic model, and do not require the project to compile. The extractor must be error-tolerant so a single malformed or partial file degrades to fewer nodes rather than aborting the whole graph.
items.applicability: The first stage of reasoning over any imported corpus, including legacy banking projects that may not build in isolation.
items.example: A file with a missing using directive still yields its file, type, and method nodes because parsing succeeds even though binding would fail.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: parse with CSharpSyntaxTree and walk syntax nodes only; never call GetSemanticModel or load referenced assemblies. Test implication: feed an uncompilable file and assert nodes are still produced and no exception escapes. Generator implication: the generator can rely on the graph existing for any corpus, but must treat edges as syntactic hints. Boundary: syntax-only means types are known by their written name, not their resolved symbol, so precision is bounded by what the source text states.

items.title: Model nodes as file, type, and method with stable identity
items.description: Model the graph with three node kinds — file, type, and method — each carrying a stable identifier, a display name, the owning file path, and a source span. Identity must be reconstructable from the source locus so the same declaration maps to the same node across re-extractions.
items.applicability: The vertex schema for every reasoning query, impact walk, and retrieval result.
items.example: Method node 'PaymentService.Post' carries its file path and line span so a retrieval hit can be rendered with a precise location.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive node ids from a deterministic locus key, not from insertion order or hash of mutable content. Test implication: re-extract the same file and assert node ids are unchanged. Generator implication: generated templates can reference nodes by stable id across runs. Boundary: overloaded methods sharing a name need the signature or span in the key to stay distinct, since name alone collides.

items.title: Use a small set of explicitly typed edges
items.description: Connect nodes only through a closed, named edge vocabulary: Contains, References, Inherits, Implements, UsesEntity, UsesDbSet, and TestCovers. Every edge declares its type so consumers can filter by relationship rather than guessing from context. New relationships require a new named edge type, not an overloaded existing one.
items.applicability: All graph traversal, impact analysis, and retrieval that needs to distinguish structural containment from behavioural dependency.
items.example: A controller node Inherits an external ControllerBase node and References a service node; impact analysis follows References but ignores Contains.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: represent edge type as an enum and reject untyped edges at insertion. Test implication: assert each producer emits the expected edge type for a known fixture. Generator implication: the generator queries by edge type, e.g. UsesDbSet to find data access. Boundary: a fixed vocabulary cannot express every relationship; relationships outside the set are simply not modelled rather than approximated.

items.title: Infer semantic roles from naming plus inheritance
items.description: Assign each type a semantic role — controller, service, dbcontext, entity, or test — by combining naming conventions (suffixes, attributes, namespace position) with inheritance evidence (base type or implemented interface). Neither signal alone is trusted; the combination raises confidence and resolves cases where a name is misleading.
items.applicability: Any reasoning step that needs to know what a type does without a semantic model, such as choosing which nodes are impact-relevant.
items.example: A type named OrdersController that Inherits ControllerBase is classified controller with high confidence; a type merely ending in 'Service' but inheriting nothing relevant is classified with lower confidence.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute roles in a dedicated pass after edges exist so inheritance is available. Test implication: assert a misnamed-but-inheriting type and a conventionally-named-but-unrelated type are classified as expected. Generator implication: role drives template selection. Boundary: roles are heuristic; an unconventional codebase with no naming discipline and no recognizable base types will yield 'unknown' rather than a wrong guess.

items.title: Compute impact as transitive incoming reference edges
items.description: Estimate the blast radius of changing a node by walking incoming reference-type edges transitively — the set of nodes that (directly or indirectly) reference the target. Containment edges are deliberately excluded from the walk because containing a thing is not depending on its behaviour.
items.applicability: Pre-change risk assessment, test selection, and explaining to a developer or generator what a proposed edit could break.
items.example: Changing a repository method returns the services that reference it, then the controllers that reference those services, but not the file that merely Contains the repository.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: traverse incoming References (and equivalent behavioural edges) with a visited set to terminate on cycles. Test implication: assert a known dependency chain is fully returned and that Contains-only neighbours are excluded. Generator implication: impact feeds the set of tests to run before accepting a patch. Boundary: because references are syntactic and by simple name, impact can over- or under-include where a name is ambiguous or a call is dynamic.

items.title: Represent out-of-corpus base types as external nodes
items.description: When a type inherits or implements something not present in the corpus (a framework base class or library interface), create an external placeholder node for it rather than dropping the relationship. The Inherits/Implements edge then has a real target, so role inference and traversal that depend on inheritance still function.
items.applicability: Any corpus that depends on frameworks whose source is not imported, which is the normal case for application code.
items.example: ControllerBase, DbContext, and Exception become external nodes; OrdersController -> Inherits -> ControllerBase(external) lets the role inferer recognise the controller.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: mark external nodes with a flag and deduplicate by qualified name so repeated references share one node. Test implication: assert inheritance from a framework type still classifies the subtype. Generator implication: external nodes signal framework boundaries the generator should not try to edit. Boundary: external nodes have no body or members, so reasoning about them is limited to the relationship that created them.

items.title: Resolve symbols by exact match before substring
items.description: When retrieving by symbol name, attempt exact-name resolution first and only fall back to substring matching if no exact node exists. Exact hits are ranked above substring hits so a precise query is not drowned by partial matches.
items.applicability: The symbol-lookup leg of hybrid retrieval, invoked whenever a query names a type or method.
items.example: Query 'Post' returns the method named exactly 'Post' ahead of 'PostPayment' and 'Repost', which are offered only as secondary substring candidates.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: index nodes by exact name in a dictionary and keep a separate scan path for substrings. Test implication: assert an exact name outranks a longer name that merely contains it. Generator implication: precise symbol queries from the generator stay precise. Boundary: substring fallback is lexical and can surface unrelated names that happen to share a token.

items.title: Hybrid retrieval fuses five independent signals
items.description: Answer a reasoning query by combining five retrieval signals: exact symbol lookup, substring scan, graph traversal from seed nodes, a deterministic knowledge index over pack items, and the experience memory of prior fixes. Each signal contributes candidates that are merged and ranked, so no single missing capability blanks the result.
items.applicability: The top-level retrieval entry point that assembles context for an answer or a generation step.
items.example: A query about a payment bug returns the PaymentService node (symbol), nearby callers (graph), a relevant posting rule (knowledge index), and a prior similar fix (experience), even with no embedding model loaded.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep each signal in its own component so it can be tested and disabled independently. Test implication: disable each signal in turn and assert the others still return results. Generator implication: retrieval works in MSSQL-only mode because no signal requires Qdrant or Ollama. Boundary: fusing lexical signals does not give semantic recall; a query using different vocabulary than the code may miss relevant nodes.

items.title: Index knowledge packs with a deterministic keyword index
items.description: Make approved knowledge-pack items retrievable through a deterministic keyword index built from item titles, tags, and descriptions. The index requires no embeddings and produces the same ranking for the same query every run, so curated knowledge is always injectable even with no model present.
items.applicability: Injecting approved project memory into reasoning context, honouring the platform rule that approved knowledge is supplied first.
items.example: A query mentioning 'journal entry balance' surfaces the balanced-voucher rule from the accounting pack via shared keywords, with stable ordering across runs.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: tokenize and weight title/tag/description fields and score by keyword overlap. Test implication: assert identical queries return identical ordered results. Generator implication: the generator can rely on curated rules being retrievable offline. Boundary: keyword overlap is lexical, so synonyms and paraphrases that share no tokens with an item will not match it.

items.title: Treat embeddings and external services as optional accelerants
items.description: The reasoning engine must produce useful results with only the deterministic signals available; embeddings, Qdrant, and Ollama are optional enhancements, never preconditions. Any code path that would call an external service must have a deterministic fallback that still returns ranked context.
items.applicability: Every retrieval and reasoning entry point, in line with the MSSQL-only operating mode of the platform.
items.example: With Qdrant and Ollama absent, retrieval still returns symbol, graph, knowledge-index, and experience hits; when present, an embedding signal is layered on top.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate external calls behind a capability check and a cached health snapshot, never a synchronous probe on the query path. Test implication: run the full reasoning flow with external services disabled and assert non-empty results. Generator implication: generation cannot block on a model being installed. Boundary: deterministic-only mode trades semantic recall for guaranteed availability; recall improves when embeddings are present.

items.title: Link tests to subjects with TestCovers edges
items.description: When a type is inferred to be a test, connect it to the production nodes it exercises through TestCovers edges, derived from the references inside the test body. This makes the inverse query — which tests guard a given node — a single edge lookup.
items.applicability: Test selection during impact analysis and explaining coverage gaps to a developer or generator.
items.example: PaymentServiceTests references PaymentService, producing TestCovers edges so a change to PaymentService immediately yields its guarding tests.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive TestCovers from the test's reference edges after role inference marks the test. Test implication: assert a known test maps to its subject and that a node with no tests reports none. Generator implication: the generator runs the covering tests before accepting a patch and flags uncovered impacted nodes. Boundary: coverage is inferred from references, not execution, so a referenced-but-not-asserted node is still reported as covered.

items.title: Map a generated file to its generator template by path
items.description: Maintain a deterministic mapping from a generated product file back to the generator template that produced it, keyed on the file path or a path convention. When reasoning encounters a defect in a generated file, this points to the template so the fix is made at the source rather than in disposable output.
items.applicability: Closing the loop between a runtime or test defect and the generator that emits the affected code.
items.example: A bug surfaced in a generated controller resolves to the controller template; the fix is applied to the template so every future generation inherits it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive the mapping from the generator's emit manifest or a stable path rule, not from content matching. Test implication: assert a generated path resolves to exactly one template. Generator implication: a GeneratorImprovement experience is recorded against the template node. Boundary: hand-edited generated files break the mapping's assumption that output is disposable; such edits must be detected and surfaced, not silently overwritten.

items.title: Aggregate reasoning context from five layers
items.description: Assemble the context for an answer or generation step from five layers: the relevant symbols, their impact set, the covering tests, the applicable approved knowledge, and any prior fixes from experience memory. The aggregate is the unit handed to a model or a deterministic responder, ensuring curated knowledge and history travel with the code.
items.applicability: The construction of every prompt context or deterministic reasoning bundle in the engine.
items.example: Answering 'how do I safely change posting?' bundles the posting symbols, the controllers impacted, the posting tests, the GL-posting rules, and the prior posting bug fix into one context object.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: build the aggregate as an explicit object with the five named sections so consumers can inspect provenance. Test implication: assert each section is populated for a query with known coverage. Generator implication: the model receives knowledge first, matching the platform's memory-first contract. Boundary: aggregation does not rank cross-layer relevance beyond each layer's own scoring, so a large impact set can crowd out other layers unless capped.

items.title: Benchmark reasoning against the real repository with no model
items.description: Validate the reasoning engine with a benchmark that runs over the actual repository graph — not a toy fixture — and scores a set of held-out questions. The benchmark must pass a defined bar (at or above 80%) with no model present, proving the deterministic signals carry the reasoning load on their own.
items.applicability: Acceptance gating for the reasoning engine and regression detection when extractors or edges change.
items.example: The V1 benchmark builds the graph over the real repo at 1308 nodes and 1699 edges and scores above 80% on its question set with embeddings and inference disabled.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: pin the benchmark to the real repo extraction and assert node/edge counts within a tolerance to catch silent extractor regressions. Test implication: fail the build if the score drops below the bar or counts shift unexpectedly. Generator implication: the score is the floor a model is expected to improve on, not replace. Boundary: a fixed question set measures only the scenarios it encodes; passing it does not prove correctness on unseen reasoning tasks.

items.title: Acknowledge that syntax-only references are by simple name
items.description: Because the extractor is syntax-only, reference edges are captured by simple identifier name rather than a resolved fully-qualified symbol. Method calls and local instantiations inside bodies are not modelled as a precise call graph. Consumers must treat references as confidence-scored hints and never assert a precise call relationship from them.
items.applicability: Any feature built on reference edges — impact analysis, cross-assembly resolution, and call-graph-flavoured queries.
items.example: Two unrelated types both named 'Logger' in different namespaces are indistinguishable to a simple-name reference, so resolution attaches a confidence rather than a certainty.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store a confidence on each reference edge and resolve simple names to candidates in a later pass. Test implication: assert ambiguous names produce multiple candidates with confidence, not a single hard edge. Generator implication: the generator weights low-confidence references accordingly and prefers high-confidence ones for risky changes. Boundary: precise call graphs require a semantic model, which is intentionally out of scope here; this design trades precision for the ability to run on any corpus offline.
